title: Why the hantavirus vaccine remains elusive
html_body:
Biotechnology, drug discovery and pharma |
View this email in your browser Wednesday 13 May 2026

Hello Nature readers,
This week, we pinpoint the foci of the pharmaceutical industry, hear the case for more drug repurposing and learn that the first of a new class of protein degrader has been approved.
An outbreak of hantavirus onboard a cruise ship has thrown a spotlight on the rare but deadly infection and the lack of options for treatment and prevention. (Ruslanas Baranauskas/Science Photo Library)

Why the hantavirus vaccine remains elusive

For more than three decades, virologist Jay Hooper has been working on a vaccine against several strains of hantavirus that can infect people, including the strain responsible for the current outbreak aboard the cruise ship MV Hondius, named the Andes virus. So far, there are no approved treatments or vaccines for the virus. “Because human cases of Andes virus are rare and geographically scattered, there is no obvious region to run a classic phase III efficacy trial, so to meet the requirements for licensing the vaccine requires more creative approaches,” Hooper tells Nature. Progress has been slow and frustrating, “like pushing a rock up a hill for years”, but Hooper and his team continue to develop antibody-based treatments and explore new vaccine technologies, such as mRNA vaccines, that could allow for faster roll out if needed.

Nature | 7 min read
Read more about the uncertainty around how hantavirus spreads (Nature | 6 min read)

A first for a new class of protein degraders

The first targeted proteolysis-targeting chimera (PROTAC) protein degrader has been approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA), 25 years after the first paper on the drug type was published. The oestrogen receptor degrader vepdegestrant (Veppanu) secured approval for a subset of patients with advanced or metastatic breast cancer. The drug is one of dozens of heterobifunctional degraders in the pipeline, but the modality has historically faced questions about safety and whether it can be delivered orally. “People have been waiting for a first approval,” says chemical biologist Craig Crews, who pioneered early research into the drug type. He expects a wave of research into new drug targets as drug developers embrace the new drug modality.

Nature Reviews Drug Discovery | 6 min read

‘Enriched’ CAR-T cells for blood cancer

Almost half of people with hard-to-treat blood cancer who were given longer-lived CAR-T cells with stem-like characteristics went into remission in a small clinical trial. The ‘enriched’ immunotherapy contained an unusually high proportion of immune cells with properties similar to stem cells, such as the ability to generate many types of T cells. “On a per-dose basis, these cells definitely seemed more potent,” says cancer immunotherapy researcher Christine Brown. “It’s a first step, but an important one.”

Nature | 5 min read
 Reference: Cell paper
Over 94 days, bone lesions shrank in one study participant following treatment with the stem-cell-like CAR-T cells. (NIH)

Approvals of the month

  • US FDA approvals in Q1: Ten approvals for new drugs marks an increase since last year, but sales are predicted to be lower. (Nature Reviews Drug Discovery | 5 min read)
  • First clinical tests of GLP-1 gene therapy given the green light in Europe: The therapy will be tested in the Netherlands and should help people with type 2 diabetes to make GLP-1 peptides. (Endpoints News | 4 min read)
  • FDA approves the first gene therapy for genetic deafness: Lunsotogene parvec-cwha (Otarmeni) gene therapy will be free for US patients. (Nature Biotechnology| 6 min read)
  • Wearable device for pancreatic cancer approved in the United States: The device uses electrical signals to disrupt cancer cell division. (Nature Biotechnology | 3 min read)
  • A new type of HIV drug gains approval: The combination of islatravir plus doravirine (Idvynso) provides a new way of stopping the transcription of the virus’ DNA when used with other antivirals. (Nature Reviews Drug Discovery | 4 min read)

Image of the week
This image of a regenerating limb bud on an African clawed frog (Xenopus laevis) tadpole helped scientists to uncover how these animals regrow severed body parts. Researchers found that exposure to low oxygen levels — similar to conditions that nascent frogs typically experience — sped up wound healing in amputated embryonic mouse limbs. The team also identified oxygen-sensing molecules involved in activating regenerative abilities in both frogs and mice.

See more of the month’s sharpest science shots, selected by Nature’s photo team. (Nature | Leisurely scroll)

Reference: Science paper (Georgios Tsissios)

Features and opinion

Where is pharma’s focus?

Over recent decades, most major drug companies have shifted their focus away from neurology and cardiovascular diseases and onto oncology and immunology, according to analysis by a group of consultants. The former has been put down to the difficulty of research and success in the areas. The latter is a response to unmet needs and rapid scientific advancement in the areas. Other ‘loud’ shifts have included movement towards cardiometabolic drugs, such as GLP-1s, leading to a spike in revenue growth from primary-care products. But the industry is also gradually building its focus on rare diseases and precision medicine. These advances rarely deliver ‘mega-blockbuster’ drugs, but contribute to a growing portfolio in a new area of medicine, write the authors.

Nature Reviews Drug Discovery | 8 min read
Increased attention on primary care products has caused a spike in the money made in the sector. But this is fueled by a small number of blockbuster drugs such as GLP-1s, rather than a broader resurgence of primary-care research and development. (Source: EvaluatePharma as of November 2025. )

Make drug repurposing the new normal

Finding new uses for existing drugs could help fill urgent treatment gaps, especially for diseases that might not attract a lot of commercial interest. But repurposing has yet to have a revolutionary impact on the existing pharmacopeia, argue attendees of this year’s International Drug Repurposing conference. “This is because the ecosystem for drug repurposing has historically been fragmented, with investigators (especially those in academia) rarely able to access the full range of expertise, experience and perspectives required to take a project from the discovery stages all the way through to regulatory approval and patient access,” write the authors. A shift will require placing patients at the centre of decisionmaking, a better understanding of disease and more collaboration with industry, among other things.

Nature Reviews Drug Discovery | 12 min read

ADVERTISEMENT FEATURE
Babis Kalodimos and his team at St. Jude revealed how hidden kinase state shapes signalling leveraging NMR technology (St. Jude Children’s Research Hospital)

Hidden kinase state shapes signalling

St. Jude researchers have identified a previously unseen conformational state in Src-family kinases that is essential for their activity, helping explain how these enzymes control T-cell signalling and cell migration. The finding suggests that even widely-studied proteins can adopt fleeting, ‘invisible’ states that conventional methods for visualizing protein structures often miss. The work provides a more complete model of kinase function and regulation, and could help guide drugs that stabilize or disrupt specific states.

Reference: Science paper

Quote of the week

“The Fifth Circuit’s approach would upend FDA’s gold-standard, science-based drug approval system, and creates a roadmap for attacks on science-based drug regulatory decisions.”

In an argument to the US Supreme Court, nine former commissioners and acting commissioners at the FDA, including Janet Woodcock and Robert Califf, argue against restricting online prescription of the contraceptive pill mifepristone after a court ruling in an appeals court limited access to in-person consultations only. (Fierce Pharma | 7 min read)

Today, I am contemplating humanity's closeness to ‘the brink’, while trying to remain hopeful that we can step back from the edge. The Doomsday Clock — established in 1947 by nuclear scientists to warn of the threat of nuclear war — is set by a group of leading scientists, academics and diplomats to indicate our closeness to the apocalypse. In January, experts set the clock to 85 seconds to midnight — the closest we’ve ever been. Their message: care about the bigger issues because if they go wrong, nothing else matters.

Please send a song you would add to the Doomsday Clock’s accompanying playlist — and your feedback — to trbriefing@nature.com.

Thanks for reading,
Laura Woodrow, editor, Nature Briefing: Translational Research
With contributions by Flora Graham

Progress has been slow and frustrating, “like pushing a rock up a hill for years”, but Hooper and his team continue to develop antibody-based treatments and explore new vaccine technologies, such as mRNA vaccines, that could allow for faster roll out if needed.

summary.overview: 이번 뉴스레터는 바이러스 백신 개발, 단백질 분해제 혁신, 암 치료법, 제약 산업 동향, 약물 재사용 전략 등 과학 분야의 주요 이슈를 다룹니다.
covered_item_titles: Why the hantavirus vaccine remains elusive
A first for a new class of protein degraders
Make drug repurposing the new normal

article_briefings.title: Why the hantavirus vaccine remains elusive
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 하안트바이러스 백신 개발이 어려운 이유를 조사한 기사로, 30년 이상 연구를 진행한 바이러스학자 조에이 호퍼의 연구와 현재 크루즈선에서 발생한 유태리아 바이러스 관련 상황을 다룹니다.

article_briefings.title: A first for a new class of protein degraders
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 첫 번째로 승인된 타겟팅 프로테올리시스-타겟팅 치머(PROTAC) 단백질 분해제가 FDA 승인을 받으며, 25년 만에 의약품 개발 분야의 혁신을 이끌고 있습니다.

article_briefings.title: ‘Enriched’ CAR-T cells for blood cancer
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 혈액암 치료에 '풍부화된' CAR-T 세포가 임상 시험에서 반응률을 높이는 데 성공했으며, 줄기세포 같은 특성을 지닌 면역세포의 역할을 강조합니다.

article_briefings.title: Where is pharma’s focus?
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 제약 산업이 최근 몇십 년간 신경학과 심혈관 질환에서 종양학 및 면역학 분야로 집중되고 있으며, 이는 치료법 개발의 어려움 때문으로 분석됩니다.

article_briefings.title: Make drug repurposing the new normal
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 기존 약물의 새로운 용도를 찾는 재사용 전략이 급성 질환 치료에 효과적이지만, 현재까지 혁신적인 결과를 내지 못하고 있다는 논의가 제기됩니다.

raw_metadata.subject: Why the hantavirus vaccine remains elusive
